does: This paper adapts clinical validity scaling frameworks from the PAI and MMPI-3 personality assessments to evaluate response validity in large language model metacognitive self-reports. The authors operationalize six validity indices measuring response distortions—including confidence maintenance on errors, betting on errors, consensus-withdrawal patterns, and response consistency issues—across 20 frontier models evaluated on 524 items. They find that four models exhibit construct-level invalidity while two show elevated concerns, with valid-profile models demonstrating item-sensitive confidence calibration (mean r = .18) whereas invalid-profile models show negative correlations (mean r = −.20, d = 2.17). Chain-of-thought training is identified as producing opposing response distortions. A two-dimensional latent structure explains 94.6% of validity index variance, establishing measurable response validity signatures across models.
connects: This work extends established clinical assessment methodology to a novel domain, building on decades of research in detecting malingering, minimization, and inconsistent responding in human psychological evaluations. The PAI and MMPI-3 validity frameworks represent mature approaches to screening confounded self-report data before interpreting substantive constructs. The paper responds to the gap in LLM evaluation protocols, where model confidence and self-assessment are increasingly used for prediction and monitoring but lack systematic validity checks. It connects to recent work on LLM calibration and metacognitive accuracy while importing the clinical insight that response patterns themselves contain diagnostic information about data quality and interpretability. The focus on latent dimensionality in validity signatures relates to factor-analytic traditions in psychometrics.
enables: This foundational work enables a two-stage evaluation framework for LLM self-report: first screening response validity, then interpreting substantive assessments only when profiles pass validity thresholds. The portable screening protocol in companion papers creates a practical tool for practitioners and researchers to rapidly classify new models. By establishing that response validity predicts meaningful differences in confidence calibration and item sensitivity, the paper supports selective prediction applications where confidence scores are used to defer uncertain predictions. The identified dimensions of model response distortion open pathways for targeted interventions—such as improved training procedures that avoid the distortions currently produced by chain-of-thought techniques. The framework also establishes a replicable methodology for detecting when LLM self-assessments become interpretable, supporting safer deployment in high-stakes domains requiring confidence-based decision-making.
